le","type":"function"},{"inputs":[{"internalType":"bytes32","name":"credentialHash","type":"bytes32"}],"name":"revokeCredential","outputs":[],"stateMutability":"nonpayable","type":"function"},{"inputs":[],"name":"totalCredentials","outputs":[{"internalType":"uint256","name":"","type":"uint256"}],"stateMutability":"view","type":"function"},{"inputs":[],"name":"universityAddress","outputs":[{"internalType":"address","name":"","type":"address"}],"stateMutability":"view","type":"function"},{"inputs":[{"internalType":"bytes32","name":"credentialHash","type":"bytes32"}],"name":"verifyCredential","outputs":[{"internalType":"bool","name":"isValid","type":"bool"},{"internalType":"bool","name":"isRevoked","type":"bool"},{"internalType":"string","name":"ipfsCid","type":"string"},{"internalType":"uint256","name":"issuedAt","type":"uint256"}],"stateMutability":"view","type":"function"}],"devdoc":{"kind":"dev","methods":{"constructor":{"details":"Called ONCE when the contract is deployed to the blockchain.","params":{"_universityAddress":"The wallet address that will issue credentials. HOW HARDHAT CALLS THIS:   In scripts/deploy.js:     const contract = await CredentialVerifier.deploy(universityAddress);   The address is passed as a constructor argument. WHY immutable?   Once set, universityAddress can NEVER change. This is a security feature.   If we allowed changing it, an attacker who compromises the contract owner   could change the university to their own address and issue fake credentials."}},"getCredential(bytes32)":{"details":"Returns full on-chain data for a credential (for admin/debugging).","params":{"credentialHash":"The hash to look up"}},"getStudentCredentials(string)":{"details":"Returns all credential hashes issued to a specific student.","params":{"studentDid":"The student's DID"},"returns":{"_0":"Array of credential hashes (bytes32[]) CALLED BY: FastAPI's get_student_credentials() endpoint NOTE: Returns hashes only \u2014 caller must fetch full data per hash using verifyCredential()"}},"issueCredential(string,bytes32,string)":{"details":"Issues a new academic credential on-chain.","params":{"credentialHash":"SHA-256 hash of the full credential JSON (as bytes32)","ipfsCid":"IPFS Content ID where the full credential is stored CALLED BY: FastAPI's store_on_blockchain() function SECURITY: onlyUniversity modifier ensures only IIT Bombay's wallet can call this GAS COST: ~80,000\u2013120,000 gas (writing to storage is expensive) WHY bytes32 for credentialHash? \u2192 SHA-256 always produces exactly 32 bytes. \u2192 bytes32 is a fixed-size Solidity type \u2014 no dynamic allocation overhead. \u2192 Cheaper gas than string for storing hashes. \u2192 In Python: bytes32 = bytes.fromhex(hash_string)","studentDid":"The student's Decentralized Identifier"}},"revokeCredential(bytes32)":{"details":"Revokes a previously issued credential.","params":{"credentialHash":"Hash of the credential to revoke REVOCATION IS NOT DELETION:   We don't delete the record \u2014 we flip is_revoked to true.   Why? Because the blockchain is immutable \u2014 you can't actually delete.   Also, the audit trail of \"this was issued and then revoked\" is valuable.   Employers can see the credential existed but was revoked."}},"verifyCredential(bytes32)":{"details":"Verifies if a credential hash is valid and not revoked.","params":{"credentialHash":"The hash to check"},"returns":{"ipfsCid":"       The IPFS CID to fetch the full credential","isRevoked":"     True if the credential was revoked","isValid":"       True if the credential exists AND is not revoked","issuedAt":"      Unix timestamp when it was issued CALLED BY: FastAPI's verify_credential() endpoint GAS COST: FREE (view function, called with .call() not .transact())"}}},"stateVariables":{"credentials":{"details":"Primary index: hash \u2192 Credential WHY bytes32 as key instead of string? \u2192 bytes32 is a fixed-size type \u2014 cheaper and simpler for Solidity. \u2192 SHA-256 hashes are always 32 bytes. Perfect fit. \u2192 string keys are dynamic and more expensive to hash internally. Solidity mapping = like a Python dict, but:   - Keys that don't exist return zero-value struct (not null/KeyError)   - You can't iterate over keys (no .keys() method)   - Perfectly efficient O(1) lookup by hash"},"studentCredentials":{"details":"Secondary index: studentDid \u2192 list of credential hashes WHY store this separately? \u2192 The primary map lets you look up ONE credential by hash (for verification). \u2192 This secondary map lets you list ALL credentials for a student (for dashboard). \u2192 Without this, you'd have to scan every credential ever issued \u2014 impossible."}},"version":1},"userdoc":{"kind":"user","methods":{},"version":1}},"settings":{"compilationTarget":{"project/contracts/CredentialVerifier.sol":"CredentialVerifier"},"evmVersion":"shanghai","libraries":{},"metadata":{"bytecodeHash":"ipfs"},"optimizer":{"enabled":true,"runs":200},"remappings":[]},"sources":{"project/contracts/CredentialVerifier.sol":{"keccak256":"0x9fdebb31d639ac8284e5cc96821dc3749385c4bf939db1667102c8ce59de8cbd","license":"MIT","urls":["bzz-raw://4b41353fb1af188f41fbf655d8d4d4a1f623807905267cd2e1022613cd0e7537","dweb:/ipfs/QmS7XqFRL8nE1SaSY1ro6XPMnAhEk94ueo6vbPjMJDiy8e"]}},"version":1}

@dev Represents one academic credential stored on-chain.
 We store MINIMAL data here — just enough to verify and look up.
 The full credential (name, degree, CGPA, etc.) lives on IPFS.
 Storage costs in Solidity:
   - bool     → 1 byte  (but padded to 32 bytes in storage slot)
   - uint256  → 32 bytes
   - address  → 20 bytes
   - string   → variable (dynamic, more expensive)
   - bytes32  → exactly 32 bytes (fixed, cheaper than string for hashes)

@dev Primary index: hash → Credential
 WHY bytes32 as key instead of string?
 → bytes32 is a fixed-size type — cheaper and simpler for Solidity.
 → SHA-256 hashes are always 32 bytes. Perfect fit.
 → string keys are dynamic and more expensive to hash internally.
 Solidity mapping = like a Python dict, but:
   - Keys that don't exist return zero-value struct (not null/KeyError)
   - You can't iterate over keys (no .keys() method)
   - Perfectly efficient O(1) lookup by hash

@dev Secondary index: studentDid → list of credential hashes
 WHY store this separately?
 → The primary map lets you look up ONE credential by hash (for verification).
 → This secondary map lets you list ALL credentials for a student (for dashboard).
 → Without this, you'd have to scan every credential ever issued — impossible.

@dev Revokes a previously issued credential.
 @param credentialHash  Hash of the credential to revoke
 REVOCATION IS NOT DELETION:
   We don't delete the record — we flip is_revoked to true.
   Why? Because the blockchain is immutable — you can't actually delete.
   Also, the audit trail of "this was issued and then revoked" is valuable.
   Employers can see the credential existed but was revoked.

 @param credentialHash  The hash to look up
